meta.title: 2.2 Server-Side Containers (Docker)
meta.description: Docker became the default because it solved a real, persistent problem: "it works on my machine." Before containers, deploying software meant managing operating system dependencies, runtime versions,

blocks.prompt:
Docker is the default answer for "run this code in isolation." What makes it the default, and when is it the wrong answer? Write down your instinct before reading.

---

blocks.content:
Docker became the default because it solved a real, persistent problem: "it works on my machine." Before containers, deploying software meant managing operating system dependencies, runtime versions, and system library differences across development, staging, and production environments. A Node.js app written against Node 16 might behave differently on a server running Node 14. Docker packages the application and its entire runtime environment into a single artifact that runs identically everywhere.

**How containers actually work** — not magic, but three Linux kernel features working together:

**Namespaces** partition system resources so that processes inside a container see an isolated view of the world. A container gets its own PID namespace (its processes start at PID 1 — process ID 1 is the first process the OS starts; containers fake this so their processes appear to be the only processes running), its own network namespace (its own network interfaces and routing table), its own mount namespace (its own filesystem view), and its own UTS namespace (its own hostname). From inside the container, it looks like a private machine.

**cgroups** (control groups — a Linux kernel feature that limits and monitors resource usage for groups of processes) enforce resource limits. A container can be constrained to 512MB of RAM and 0.5 CPU cores. If it tries to allocate more, the kernel intervenes — the process gets killed or throttled. This is the mechanism that prevents one noisy tenant from degrading the whole host.

**Layered filesystem** (OverlayFS — a filesystem that stacks multiple layers so multiple containers can share a read-only base layer while each writes only to their own top layer) allows Docker images to share base layers. Your Python application image might be 150MB, but 100MB of that is the base Ubuntu layer that dozens of other images also use. On disk, that 100MB is stored once. Each `RUN` command in a Dockerfile creates a new immutable layer. Only the top writable layer differs per container instance. This makes images cheap to store and fast to distribute.

**The Dockerfile → Image → Container lifecycle:**

A `Dockerfile` is a recipe. `docker build` executes that recipe to produce an immutable **image**. A **container** is a running instance of an image — the same image can run as hundreds of containers simultaneously, each with its own writable layer. `docker push` ships the image to a registry. `docker run` instantiates a container from the image.

**Why containers are excellent for production workloads:** They're reproducible (the image is immutable), portable (same image runs on AWS, GCP, on-prem), auditable (the Dockerfile is readable), and well-understood by the industry. Every major cloud platform has container orchestration (systems like Kubernetes that manage many containers automatically — scheduling, scaling, health checks) built in. A decade of tooling exists around them.

---

blocks.question: A Docker container uses namespaces, cgroups, and a layered filesystem. Which of these three is responsible for enforcing the memory limit that prevents one container from crashing the entire host?
blocks.answer: cgroups (control groups) are the resource-enforcement mechanism. Namespaces create the isolation illusion — each container sees a private view of processes and files. The layered filesystem enables efficient image sharing. But cgroups are what enforce hard limits on CPU, memory, and I/O. When a container exceeds its memory ceiling, the kernel's OOM killer terminates that container's process — not processes in other containers. This is the layer that turns 'isolation' from a visibility property into a resource-enforcement property.

blocks.content:
**Why containers are problematic for Make's preview use case:**

The critical issue is **cold start time** (the delay from requesting a new environment to it being ready to serve requests). When a user edits their prompt and expects to see a preview update, the container lifecycle becomes the enemy. A minimal Node.js container takes 2-4 seconds to start. A container with a full Next.js app and its `node_modules` can take 5-10 seconds. That's before the application itself has booted.

The second issue is **resource overhead**. Each running container consumes meaningful memory — a Node.js process alone is ~50-100MB resident. At 10,000 concurrent users, each with an active preview environment, you're looking at 500GB-1TB of RAM just for baseline process overhead. The cost scales linearly with concurrent sessions.

The third issue is **session management complexity**. Containers are stateful. When a user's session goes idle, do you keep the container running (expensive), checkpoint it (complex), or terminate it (cold start on resume)? None of these options are clean.

**Container orchestration** (Kubernetes — a platform that automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications) solves operational problems like health checking, rolling deployments, and bin-packing across nodes — but it adds its own layer of complexity. Kubernetes startup overhead (scheduler latency, image pull, kubelet reconciliation) adds additional seconds to cold start. For a batch processing workload, this is acceptable. For an interactive preview tool, it's product-killing.

**When containers ARE right for Make:**

Containers remain the right choice for production deployments of apps generated by Make. When a user says "deploy this to production," they want a reliable, long-running environment — cold start doesn't matter because the container starts once and runs for weeks. Containers are also right for long-running development sessions where the amortized startup cost is acceptable, and for code that requires native system dependencies that can't run in a browser sandbox.

The pattern that emerges: containers for production deployments, lighter-weight isolation mechanisms for interactive previews.

---

blocks.question: An engineer proposes solving Make's cold start problem by pre-building Docker images for the five most common project templates (React + Vite, Next.js, etc.) and caching them on every host machine. A user starting a project from a template would skip the image pull. What does this solve, and what doesn't it solve?
blocks.answer:
This solves image pull latency (the time to download the image from a registry) but doesn't solve container startup latency (the time for namespaces to initialize, the process to start, the Node.js runtime to load, and the dev server to begin listening). Template projects also diverge as soon as the user installs packages, invalidating the cached image. Pre-built images help at the margins but don't address the fundamental cost and startup overhead problems.*

---

blocks.title:
Pull and run a Node.js container locally:

```bash
docker pull node:18-alpine
time docker run --rm node:18-alpine node -e "console.log('ready')"
```

Note the elapsed time. Now run it a second time (image already pulled) and compare. Then run:

```bash
time docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/app -w /app node:18-alpine sh -c "npm install && node index.js"
```

with a simple Express app. This is the realistic interactive preview scenario. The time you observe is your cold start budget.

---
blocks.solution: On the first `docker run` after pull, you'll typically see 400ms–2 seconds depending on your machine — even though the image is already on disk, the container still needs to initialize namespaces, start the process, and execute Node.js. On the second run (image cached, no pull needed), the time should be similar or slightly faster — confirming that image pull is not the dominant cost; container startup is. When you add `npm install`, expect 5–30 seconds depending on your project's dependencies and network speed. This full sequence — pull → start container → install deps — is what every cold-start preview environment goes through. If your total was under 3 seconds, you have a fast machine and a small package.json. If it was 10+ seconds, that's the experience a user on a cold session would have before seeing anything. Notice that the image-caching optimization the module discusses would only shave off the pull time, not the initialization and install time.

blocks.prompt:
Explain to a CFO in three sentences why "just run every user's preview in a Docker container" would be financially unsustainable at 50,000 monthly active users.

---

questions:
Recall a product or feature you worked on where the infrastructure choice created a feedback-loop problem for users — where the time between action and visible result was long enough to cause confusion or drop-off. What was driving that latency, and was it ever addressed?

---

blocks.content:
Containers are the right isolation primitive for long-running, production-grade workloads, but their cold start times and linear cost scaling make them architecturally mismatched with interactive preview environments where sub-2-second feedback is a product requirement.

---

blocks.prompt:
If containers are too slow and too expensive for interactive preview, what would it mean to run the code execution entirely inside the user's browser tab? What would you need to fake or emulate to make that possible?

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: Docker uses three Linux kernel features — namespaces, cgroups, and layered filesystem — to create container isolation. Which of these is most directly responsible for preventing one container from accessing another container's processes or files?
options: cgroups, because they enforce resource limits that prevent one container from consuming resources needed by another
The layered filesystem (OverlayFS), because separate layers for each container prevent cross-container file access
Namespaces, because they partition system resources so each container sees an isolated view — its own PID namespace, network namespace, mount namespace, and filesystem namespace
reviewQuestions.explanation: Namespaces are the isolation primitive. A container's PID namespace means it sees its own process tree starting at PID 1 — it cannot see host processes or other containers' processes. Its mount namespace means it sees only its own filesystem view. cgroups handle resource limits (how much CPU/RAM) not process visibility. OverlayFS enables efficient image sharing on disk but doesn't create process or filesystem isolation — it's about storage efficiency.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — namespaces create the isolation boundary; cgroups enforce resource limits within it. Distinguishing these is essential for evaluating container security claims.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: cgroups control resource consumption (CPU throttling, memory limits) — they don't prevent one container from seeing another's processes. OverlayFS is about storage efficiency on disk — shared base layers don't create runtime isolation. Namespaces are the mechanism that makes containers appear to be isolated machines by giving each its own view of PIDs, network, and filesystem.

reviewQuestions.question: An engineer proposes solving Make's preview cold start problem by pre-building Docker images for the five most common templates and caching them on every host. What does this optimization address, and what does it leave unsolved?
options: This optimization doesn't help because Docker images are already cached at the OS level after the first pull, so this is redundant with existing behavior
This optimization solves both image pull latency and container startup latency — the container is effectively ready to serve requests as soon as the cache check completes

This solves image pull latency (the download from registry), but not container startup latency (namespace initialization, Node.js process startup, and dev server ready time) — which still takes 2-10 seconds
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module explicitly breaks down where cold start time goes: image pull is one stage, container initialization (creating namespaces, the process starting, Node.js loading, the dev server listening) is a separate and still-costly stage. Pre-caching images eliminates the pull latency but leaves the initialization cost unchanged. Template images also diverge immediately when users install packages, invalidating the cache. This is why the module frames pre-built images as a marginal improvement, not a solution.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Correct — this distinction between pull latency and startup latency is critical for evaluating infrastructure proposals, and the module uses exactly this example.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Docker doesn't automatically cache images globally at the OS level across all hosts — each host must pull the image independently unless it's pre-staged. More importantly, even with the image cached, container startup still takes time: namespace creation, process initialization, Node.js loading, and dev server startup. The pre-built image optimization is real but only addresses one stage of the cold start pipeline.

reviewQuestions.question: The module concludes that containers are right for production deployments but architecturally mismatched with interactive previews. What is the specific property of interactive previews that makes containers the wrong choice?
options: Containers are too expensive for production deployments, which is why they're relegated to previews — the economics only work when costs are shared across users

Interactive previews require sub-2-second feedback from prompt to visible preview; container cold starts of 2-10 seconds violate this requirement, and the per-session memory overhead makes the cost model unsustainable at scale
Containers can't display visual output in a browser, so they're inherently unsuitable for any preview use case regardless of startup time
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module is explicit: containers are right for long-running production workloads where startup cost is amortized over weeks of uptime. They're wrong for interactive previews because the feedback loop requirement (sub-2-second from code change to visible preview) is violated by 2-10 second cold starts, and because the per-session memory overhead (50-100MB per Node.js process) creates unsustainable costs at scale. The module explicitly recommends containers for production and lighter-weight isolation for previews.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — the interactive feedback loop requirement is the specific product property that makes containers architecturally mismatched for previews, regardless of their other strengths.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The economics actually work better for containers in long-running production scenarios, not previews — the module argues the opposite. Containers do display content to browsers just fine via port-forwarding. The mismatch is specifically about interactive latency: the product promise of 'instant preview' cannot be fulfilled by a system with 2-10 second cold starts.
